elements.section-2411-006: Resource estimate for Hamiltonian time evolution: Suzuki--Trotter decomposition algorithm

elements.sent-2411-257: Given the rapid pace of innovation in quantum computing hardware and software, accurately predicting the performance of an orbifold lattice-based code on a quantum computer by, say, 2030, is challenging.

elements.sent-2411-293: The combination of CNOT gates and single-qubit rotation gates forms a universal gate set, enabling the construction of any quantum algorithm using only these gates.
elements.sent-2411-294: This gate set is especially important in the NISQ era, where quantum computations are performed on physical qubits without the benefit of error correction.
elements.sent-2411-295: In NISQ devices, all gates are implemented directly on the physical qubits.
elements.sent-2411-296: Note that there exist different physical implementations of qubit gates and some platforms, like the H-series hardware by Quantinuum~\cite{quantinuum2024 , can implement directly arbitrary angle two-qubit gates $e^{-i\theta_{pq}\hat{\sigma _{z,p}\hat{\sigma _{z,q }$ using a single laser pulse.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-297: Without error corrections, the fidelity of quantum gates becomes a critical factor in determining how well a computation can be performed.
elements.sent-2411-298: Notably, the fidelity of two-qubit gates, like the CNOT gate, is typically much lower than that of single-qubit gates.
elements.sent-2411-299: As a result, errors accumulate more rapidly when a quantum circuit relies heavily on two-qubit gates, which can significantly degrade the overall performance of the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-300: Given the lower fidelity associated with two-qubit gates, it is reasonable to consider the number of CNOT gates as a key computational resource when assessing the efficiency and accuracy of quantum simulations in the NISQ regime.
elements.sent-2411-301: The CNOT gate count serves as a useful metric for estimating how error-prone a quantum algorithm might be on current hardware.
elements.sent-2411-302: By minimizing the number of CNOT gates in a circuit, we can reduce the potential for error accumulation, thereby improving the fidelity of the computation.
elements.sent-2411-303: In FTQC, the computational paradigm shifts significantly compared to NISQ systems.
elements.sent-2411-304: In FTQC, error correction is employed through the use of logical qubits, which are constructed from multiple physical qubits.
elements.sent-2411-305: This allows for the protection of quantum information from noise and errors, enabling more robust and scalable quantum computations.
elements.sent-2411-306: As a result, the focus on computational cost changes: the number of CNOT gates, which plays a critical role in NISQ systems, is no longer the primary factor determining the computational expense.
elements.sent-2411-307: The most commonly used universal gate set in FTQC is the combination of Clifford gates and the T gates.\footnote{ The T gate is defined by \begin{equation}\hat{T} = \left( {cc} 1 0 0 \exp(\frac{i\pi}{4})  \right)\, . \nonumber\end{equation} Clifford gates, including the Hadamard, phase, and CNOT gates, are generally not resource-intensive in various error-correcting codes, such as the surface code~\cite{FowlerSurfaceReview .}}
elements.sent-2411-308: They can often be implemented efficiently through techniques like code deformation and lattice surgery~\cite{Horsman_2012,Herr_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-309: Additionally, Clifford gates can be pushed to the end of a quantum circuit, where they can be seamlessly absorbed into Pauli measurements~\cite{Litinski2019gameofsurfacecodes , further optimizing resource usage.}
elements.sent-2411-310: In contrast, non-Clifford gates, such as the T gate, are typically much more resource-intensive.
elements.sent-2411-311: A fault-tolerant implementation of the T gate often involves gate teleportation, which relies on specialized resource states known as magic states~\cite{BravyiKitaevMagic, KnillMagic .}
elements.sent-2411-312: However, producing high-fidelity magic states is considerably costly.
elements.sent-2411-313: As a result, the T gate count has become a standard metric for estimating resource requirements in FTQC.\footnote{There has been significant progress in improving the efficiency of magic state distillation.}
elements.sent-2411-314: For instance, see the recent advancements in Ref.~\cite{Gidney_Cultivation .}
elements.sent-2411-315: In order to switch from the universal gate set $\{$CNOT, single qubit rotations$\}$ to $\{$Clifford, T$\}$, each rotation gate -- specifically the $R_Z$ gate, $R_Z(\theta)=\exp\left(-\frac{\mathrm{i}\theta {2}\hat{\sigma _z\right)$ -- needs to be approximated using a combination of T-gates and single-qubit Clifford gates.}}
